title: Administrative Obedience: Carrying Out Orders to Use Psychological-Administrative Violence
citation: Meeus, W. H. J., & Raaijmakers, Q. A. W. (1986). Administrative obedience: Carrying out orders to use psychological-administrative violence. European Journal of Social Psychology, 16(4), 311-324.

key_findings.main_effect: 92% obedience rate in administrative violence condition
key_findings.comparison_to_milgram: Higher obedience than Milgram's electric shock paradigm
key_findings.control_condition: No stressful remarks in control without authority pressure
key_findings.robustness: Demonstrates obedience extends beyond physical harm to administrative/psychological harm

critical_elements: Psychological harm (job applicant stress) rather than physical harm
Clear observable stress signals from applicant
common_pitfalls: Making harm too abstract or unrealistic
ethical_considerations: Requires thorough debriefing about confederate nature
Modern IRB approval may be challenging
Consider using hypothetical scenarios for LLMs
